summary: American pragmatist philosopher, educator, and public intellectual. Author of Democracy and Education, Art as Experience, and The Public and Its Problems. Founded the University of Chicago Laboratory Schools. Called his philosophy 'instrumentalism.'

works.name: Democracy and Education
works.description: Dewey's magnum opus (1916) on educational philosophy, arguing that democracy depends on how education conditions people to be part of the community. It opens by noting that 'unlike inanimate things, living things maintain themselves through renewal.' The work argues that schools should be part of civic life, groups geography with history, defines communication as 'sharing experience until it becomes a common possession,' and synthesizes the educational philosophies of Plato and Rousseau's Emile.

clues.clue: argues democracy depends on education conditioning people for community
examples: name this author of Democracy and Education
a work claiming that the formation of the mind resulted from an interplay between the individual and community

clues.clue: opens by noting living things 'maintain themselves through renewal'

clues.clue: groups geography with history; communication is 'sharing experience'

clues.clue: synthesizes Plato's theories on childhood and Rousseau's Emile
examples: this man updated Plato's theories on childhood and Rousseau's Emile

works.name: The Public and Its Problems
works.description: Dewey's 1927 response to Walter Lippmann's The Phantom Public. While Lippmann argued that the public is an illusion incapable of meaningful democratic participation, Dewey countered that the public is not a 'phantom' but comes into being when threatened by negative external forces, and that widespread communication could form a 'Great Community.' Dewey argued that technology distracts the public from political issues, creating an 'eclipse' of the public.

clues.clue: response to Walter Lippmann's The Phantom Public — public is not a 'phantom' but in 'eclipse'
examples: reacting against Walter Lippman, this philosopher argued for a political order
a response to Walter Lippmann in The Public and its Problems

clues.clue: widespread communication necessary for 'Great Community'

clues.clue: public comes into being only when threatened by 'negative externalities'
examples: the first title entity is created only in response to 'negative externalities'

clues.clue: technology distracts the public from political issues
examples: this philosopher bemoaned the fact that technology distracts the title group from political issues

works.name: Art as Experience
works.description: Dewey's major work on aesthetics (1934), delivered as the first William James Lectures at Harvard. It argues that artistic objects should not be separated from 'conditions of origin and operation in experience' and that there is a continuity between aesthetics and everyday life. The 'live creature' is a central concept — a being situated in and interacting with its environment. The work challenges the 'museum' conception of art, citing Asian and African artifacts as objects with both aesthetic and instrumental functions.

clues.clue: continuity between aesthetics and everyday life; challenges 'museum' conception of art
examples: the purpose of aesthetics is to reforge the connection between everyday life and fine art
this text challenges the 'museum' conception of its first title subject matter

clues.clue: art product is not identical with the work of art — arises from engagement with the object
examples: a work of art is not identical with the 'art product,' or the physical object, but arises out of the 'Live Creature's' engagement

clues.clue: Asian and African artifacts as aesthetic objects with instrumental functions

works.name: The Reflex Arc Concept in Psychology
works.description: Dewey's influential 1896 paper in Psychological Review that challenged the sequential stimulus-response model of behavior. This paper established Dewey's scientific credentials and contributed to the 'perception-action cycle' that influenced Donald Hebb's neuroscience and the later Head Start program.

clues.clue: challenged stimulus-response / reflex arc model in 1896 Psychological Review article
examples: this thinker criticized models of human behavior as sequential stimulus-response in an essay on the 'reflex arc' model
challenged the stimulus-response model in an early Psychological Review article

clues.clue: influenced Donald Hebb's neuroscience and the 'perception-action cycle'
examples: this American psychologist's paper criticizing 'The Reflex-Arc Concept in Psychology'... Hebb's insight flowed from the idea of the 'perception-action cycle'

works.name: General / Biographical & Political Activity
works.description: Dewey founded the University of Chicago Laboratory Schools to promote experiential, progressive learning. He called his philosophy 'instrumentalism' and wrote Knowing and the Known with Arthur Bentley. He led a commission that sought to exonerate Leon Trotsky during the Moscow Trials. He spent two years in China lecturing, with his lectures back-translated from Chinese. Bertrand Russell warned Dewey's philosophy would contribute to 'cosmic impiety.' Richard Rorty praised Dewey as embodying the American spirit. Sidney Hook was known as 'Dewey's bulldog.' Dewey's 'My Pedagogic Creed' is a five-article creed.

comprehensive_summary:
John Dewey is the American pragmatist philosopher best known for his work in education, aesthetics, and political philosophy. His magnum opus, Democracy and Education (1916), argues that democratic society depends on education conditioning people for community participation. It synthesizes Plato and Rousseau's educational thought and defines communication as 'sharing experience until it becomes a common possession.'

The Public and Its Problems (1927) is Dewey's response to Walter Lippmann's The Phantom Public. While Lippmann argued the public is an illusion, Dewey countered that the public is in 'eclipse' rather than phantom, and that widespread communication could form a 'Great Community.' Dewey argued technology distracts the public from political engagement.

Art as Experience (1934), delivered as the first William James Lectures at Harvard, argues for continuity between aesthetics and everyday life, centered on the concept of the 'live creature' interacting with its environment. It challenges the 'museum' conception of art by citing Asian and African artifacts that serve both aesthetic and instrumental functions.

Dewey's 1896 paper 'The Reflex Arc Concept in Psychology' challenged the stimulus-response model and influenced Donald Hebb's neuroscience. He founded the University of Chicago Laboratory Schools to promote experiential learning and called his philosophy 'instrumentalism.' He led a commission to exonerate Leon Trotsky during the Moscow Trials, spent two years lecturing in China, and wrote Knowing and the Known with Arthur Bentley. Richard Rorty praised Dewey as embodying the American spirit in Achieving Our Country, and Sidney Hook was known as 'Dewey's bulldog.' Cornel West discussed Dewey's reframing of 'givens' as 'takens' in The American Evasion of Philosophy, presaging Wilfrid Sellars's 'Myth of the Given.'

cards.front: Work: responds to Walter Lippmann's The Phantom Public by arguing the public is in 'eclipse' rather than phantom, and that communication can form a 'Great Community'
cards.back: The Public and Its Problems (John Dewey)

cards.front: Work: argues for continuity between aesthetics and everyday life, centering on the concept of the 'live creature' interacting with its environment
Work: challenges the 'museum' conception of art by citing Asian and African artifacts that served both aesthetic and instrumental functions
Philosopher: challenged the sequential stimulus-response model in a 1896 Psychological Review article about the 'reflex arc,' influencing Donald Hebb's neuroscience
cards.work: The Reflex Arc Concept in Psychology

cards.front: Philosopher: led a commission that sought to exonerate Leon Trotsky during the Moscow Trials, and spent two years lecturing in China with lectures back-translated from Chinese
Philosopher: Cornel West praised this thinker for presciently rejecting 'givens' in favor of 'takens,' presaging Wilfrid Sellars's 'Myth of the Given'
Philosopher: Bertrand Russell warned this thinker's philosophy would contribute to the danger of 'cosmic impiety' in the penultimate chapter of the History of Western Philosophy
Philosopher: wrote Knowing and the Known with Arthur Bentley and distinguished between primary 'had' and reflective 'known' interactions with reality
Philosopher: Frederick Taylor's scientific management and this philosopher's ideas inspired the 'platoons' of the Gary Plan for education
